44	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Checker

46	You compare **one Lean file** against **its blueprint chapter** and report whether the Lean follows the blueprint faithfully. You are read-only.

48	## Your Job
49	
50	Your directive names one `.lean` file (e.g. `Algebra/WLocal.lean`) and its corresponding blueprint chapter (e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Algebra_WLocal.tex`). You audit **bidirectionally**:
51	
52	### (A) Does the Lean follow the blueprint?
53	
54	For each `\lean{...}`-tagged block in the chapter, verify the Lean side:
55	
56	- **Declaration exists** with the right name (matches `\lean{namespace.theorem_name}`).
57	- **Signature matches** the informal statement in the chapter — the types are what the prose says they should be. A statement that type-checks but uses the wrong type class (e.g. `Smooth f` where the prose says "smooth of relative dimension n") is **wrong**, not "approximate".
58	- **Proof follows the blueprint sketch** — the proof in the Lean file should be a formalization of the steps in the chapter's `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}`, not a different argument that happens to produce the same conclusion.
59	- **No fake / placeholder statements** — definitions or theorems whose body is just `:= sorry` or a trivially-true tautology while the chapter prose claims something substantive.
60	- **No "temporary" definitions left in place** — comments like `-- TODO: replace with the real definition`, `-- placeholder until we figure out X`, or definitions whose body is suspicious (e.g. `def Foo := True`, weakened-wrong definitions standing in for the real concept).
61	- **No comments that explain away wrong code** — "we are using a wrong definition for now", "this proof is incomplete but will be fixed", etc. These are red flags, not acceptable workflow notes.
62	
63	### (B) Is the blueprint adequate for this Lean file?
64	
65	The blueprint can also be the failure mode. For the chapter you're reading, check:
66	
67	- **Every Lean declaration in this file should map to a blueprint block** (via `\lean{...}`) OR be an explicitly-helper-only declaration. If the file has substantive declarations the blueprint doesn't reference at all, the chapter is incomplete.
68	- **Proof sketches in the blueprint should be detailed enough** for a prover to formalize correctly. If you see Lean proofs that clearly went through significant reasoning the blueprint's `\begin{proof}` does NOT preview, flag the chapter as under-specified.
69	- **`\lean{...}` hints should pin the Lean target precisely.** If the blueprint says "smooth morphism" without specifying which Mathlib predicate (`Smooth` vs `SmoothOfRelativeDimension n` vs `IsSmooth`), and the Lean file ended up with the wrong one, that's BOTH a Lean error AND a blueprint clarity failure. Flag both sides.
70	- **Definitions in the blueprint should be at the right level of generality** for the Lean to consume. If the blueprint defines something narrower than the project actually needs and the Lean had to write a parallel API to cover the gap, that's a blueprint adequacy failure.
71	
72	Both directions matter. Don't excuse a wrong Lean statement by saying "the blueprint is loose"; don't excuse a thin blueprint by saying "the Lean works anyway." Report both.

181	  - Placeholder body (`:= sorry`, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial claim, suspect `:= Classical.choice _` patterns) on a declaration the blueprint claims is substantive.
182	  - Signature mismatch with the blueprint's prose — including using the wrong Mathlib predicate (e.g. `Smooth` vs `SmoothOfRelativeDimension n`) when the prose pins one specific predicate.

184	  - Axioms or `Classical.choice _` on substantive claims the blueprint does NOT authorize.
185	  - Blueprint adequacy failure: chapter is so under-specified that a prover could not have formalized this file correctly from prose alone.

187	- **major** — partial signature mismatches that are fixable in-place, missing `\lean{...}` references to declarations that the blueprint should reference, comments that are stale but not actively misleading.
188	- **minor** — naming drift, helpers worth promoting to blueprint blocks, low-impact prose-vs-Lean differences.

44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

6	import Mathlib
7	
8	/-!
9	# Stalk of a tensor product of presheaves of modules (ingredient d.2)
10	
11	This file constructs the *comparison map* underlying the varying-ring stalk--tensor
12	commutation isomorphism
13	
14	  `(A ⊗ᵖ B).stalk x ≅ A.stalk x ⊗_{R.stalk x} B.stalk x`
15	
16	for presheaves of modules over the structure presheaf `R` of a scheme — the single
17	genuinely Mathlib-absent ingredient ("d.2") on which the unconditional associator of
18	the relative-Picard tensor substrate rests.
19	
20	This iteration builds the forward additive comparison map `stalkTensorDesc` and its
21	germ characterisation (the colimit-descent of the sectionwise base-change maps). The
22	full isomorphism `PresheafOfModules.stalkTensorIso` (blueprint `lem:stalk_tensor_commutation`)
23	additionally requires the `R.stalk x`-linear packaging of this map, the reverse map, and
24	their mutual inversion on germs — see the handoff in `task_results`.
25	
26	The construction proceeds bottom-up:
27	
28	* `PresheafOfModules.stalkTensorBilin` — for a neighbourhood `U ∋ x`, the `R(U)`-balanced
29	  bilinear map `A(U) × B(U) → A_x ⊗_{R_x} B_x`, `(a,b) ↦ germ a ⊗ germ b`.
30	* `PresheafOfModules.stalkTensorDescU` — its descent through `TensorProduct.liftAddHom`
31	  to a map `(A ⊗ᵖ B)(U) = A(U) ⊗_{R(U)} B(U) → A_x ⊗_{R_x} B_x`.
32	* `PresheafOfModules.stalkTensorDesc` — the colimit descent to the comparison map
33	  `(A ⊗ᵖ B).stalk x → A_x ⊗_{R_x} B_x`.
34	
35	Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma `lemma-stalk-tensor-product`.
36	
37	Blueprint: `chapters/Picard_TensorObjSubstrate.tex`, §`sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor`
38	(`lem:stalk_tensor_commutation`).
39	-/
40	
41	set_option autoImplicit false
42	
43	universe u
44	
45	open CategoryTheory Limits MonoidalCategory TopologicalSpace TopCat.Presheaf Opposite
46	
47	namespace PresheafOfModules
48	
49	open PresheafOfModules.Monoidal
50	
51	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — stalk of a tensor product (d.2) -/
52	
53	section StalkTensor
54	
55	variable {X : TopCat.{u}} {R : X.Presheaf CommRingCat.{u}}
56	  (A B : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _)) (x : X)
57	
58	local notation3 "Tgt" =>
59	  TensorProduct ↑(R.stalk x) ↑(TopCat.Presheaf.stalk A.presheaf x)
60	    ↑(TopCat.Presheaf.stalk B.presheaf x)
61	
62	/-- **The `R(U)`-balanced bilinear comparison map.** For a neighbourhood `U ∋ x`, the
63	bilinear (additive-monoid-hom) map `A(U) × B(U) → A_x ⊗_{R_x} B_x` sending
64	`(a, b)` to `germ a ⊗ germ b`. Project-local: the building block of the d.2 stalk--tensor
65	comparison; Mathlib has no varying-ring stalk--tensor commutation. -/
66	noncomputable def stalkTensorBilin (U : Opens X) (hx : x ∈ U) :
67	    (↑(A.obj (op U)) : Type u) →+ (↑(B.obj (op U)) : Type u) →+ Tgt where
68	  toFun m :=
69	    ((TensorProduct.mk ↑(R.stalk x) _ _
70	      ((ConcreteCategory.hom (TopCat.Presheaf.germ A.presheaf U x hx)) m)).toAddMonoidHom).comp
71	      (ConcreteCategory.hom (TopCat.Presheaf.germ B.presheaf U x hx))
72	  map_zero' := by ext n; simp
73	  map_add' m m' := by ext n; simp
74	
75	/-- The defining balancing identity making `stalkTensorBilin` descend through the
76	relative tensor product `A(U) ⊗_{R(U)} B(U)`: `(r • a) ⊗ b ↦ a ⊗ (r • b)`. -/
77	lemma stalkTensorBilin_balanced (U : Opens X) (hx : x ∈ U) (r : ↑(R.obj (op U)))
78	    (m : ↑(A.obj (op U))) (n : ↑(B.obj (op U))) :
79	    (stalkTensorBilin A B x U hx (r • m)) n = (stalkTensorBilin A B x U hx m) (r • n) := by
80	  simp only [stalkTensorBilin, AddMonoidHom.coe_mk, ZeroHom.coe_mk, AddMonoidHom.coe_comp,
81	    LinearMap.toAddMonoidHom_coe, Function.comp_apply, TensorProduct.mk_apply]
82	  rw [PresheafOfModules.germ_smul A x U hx]
83	  erw [PresheafOfModules.germ_smul B x U hx]
84	  exact TensorProduct.smul_tmul _ _ _
85	
86	/-- **The per-neighbourhood descended comparison map** `(A ⊗ᵖ B)(U) → A_x ⊗_{R_x} B_x`,
87	obtained from `stalkTensorBilin` via `TensorProduct.liftAddHom`. The source object is
88	`(A ⊗ᵖ B).presheaf.obj (op U)`, definitionally `A(U) ⊗_{R(U)} B(U)`. -/
89	noncomputable def stalkTensorDescU (U : Opens X) (hx : x ∈ U) :
90	    (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf.obj (op U) ⟶ AddCommGrpCat.of Tgt :=
91	  AddCommGrpCat.ofHom
92	    (TensorProduct.liftAddHom (stalkTensorBilin A B x U hx) (stalkTensorBilin_balanced A B x U hx))
93	
94	@[simp] lemma stalkTensorDescU_tmul (U : Opens X) (hx : x ∈ U)
95	    (a : ↑(A.obj (op U))) (b : ↑(B.obj (op U))) :
96	    (stalkTensorDescU A B x U hx) (a ⊗ₜ[R.obj (op U)] b)
97	      = (ConcreteCategory.hom (TopCat.Presheaf.germ A.presheaf U x hx)) a ⊗ₜ[↑(R.stalk x)]
98	        (ConcreteCategory.hom (TopCat.Presheaf.germ B.presheaf U x hx)) b := by
99	  change (TensorProduct.liftAddHom (stalkTensorBilin A B x U hx)
100	    (stalkTensorBilin_balanced A B x U hx)) (a ⊗ₜ[R.obj (op U)] b) = _
101	  rw [TensorProduct.liftAddHom_tmul]
102	  rfl
103	
104	/-- **Scalar compatibility of the per-neighbourhood descent.** For `r : R(U)` and a
105	section `z` of `A ⊗ᵖ B` over `U`, the descent `stalkTensorDescU` carries the `R(U)`-scalar
106	action over to the `R.stalk x`-action by the germ of `r`. This is stage (iii)'s
107	section-level core; the carrier-duality between the `CommRingCat` scalar `r : R(U)` and the
108	`RingCat`-carrier module structure on `A(U) ⊗ B(U)` is absorbed by `germ_smul`/`smul_tmul'`. -/
109	lemma stalkTensorDescU_smul (U : Opens X) (hx : x ∈ U) (r : ↑(R.obj (op U)))
110	    (z : ↑((Monoidal.tensorObj A B).obj (op U))) :
111	    (stalkTensorDescU A B x U hx) (r • z)
112	      = (ConcreteCategory.hom (TopCat.Presheaf.germ R U x hx)) r •
113	          (stalkTensorDescU A B x U hx) z := by
114	  -- The section tensor `A(U) ⊗ B(U)` is a module over the `RingCat` carrier
115	  -- `S := (R ⋙ forget₂).obj (op U)`, which is defeq to the `CommRingCat` carrier `R(U)`.
116	  -- We pin `smul_tmul'`/`smul_add`/`smul_zero` to that ring so the scalar `r` slots in.
117	  induction z using TensorProduct.induction_on with
118	  | zero =>
119	      simp only [presheaf_obj_coe, Functor.comp_obj, CommRingCat.forgetToRingCat_obj,
120	        Monoidal.tensorObj_obj, map_zero, smul_zero]
121	      exact map_zero _
122	  | tmul a b =>
123	      erw [stalkTensorDescU_tmul, stalkTensorDescU_tmul, PresheafOfModules.germ_smul A x U hx]
124	      exact (TensorProduct.smul_tmul' _ _ _).symm
125	  | add p q hp hq =>
126	      erw [smul_add]
127	      refine (map_add _ _ _).trans ?_
128	      rw [hp, hq, ← smul_add]
129	      exact congrArg _ (map_add _ p q).symm
130	
131	/-- **The d.2 comparison map** `(A ⊗ᵖ B).stalk x → A_x ⊗_{R_x} B_x`, obtained by descending
132	the per-neighbourhood maps `stalkTensorDescU` through the colimit defining the stalk. -/
133	noncomputable def stalkTensorDesc :
134	    TopCat.Presheaf.stalk (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf x ⟶ AddCommGrpCat.of Tgt :=
135	  colimit.desc ((OpenNhds.inclusion x).op ⋙ (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf)
136	    ⟨AddCommGrpCat.of Tgt,
137	      fun U => stalkTensorDescU A B x (unop U).1 (unop U).2,
138	      by
139	        intro U V i
140	        apply AddCommGrpCat.hom_ext
141	        ext z
142	        induction z using TensorProduct.induction_on with
143	        | zero => exact (AddMonoidHom.map_zero _).trans (AddMonoidHom.map_zero _).symm
144	        | tmul a b =>
145	            simp only [CategoryTheory.comp_apply, Functor.const_obj_map]
146	            erw [tensorObj_map_tmul, stalkTensorDescU_tmul, stalkTensorDescU_tmul]
147	            erw [germ_res_apply, germ_res_apply]
148	            rfl
149	        | add p q hp hq =>
150	            exact (AddMonoidHom.map_add _ p q).trans
151	              ((congrArg₂ HAdd.hAdd hp hq).trans (AddMonoidHom.map_add _ p q).symm)⟩
152	
153	/-- The comparison map `stalkTensorDesc` factors the per-neighbourhood map
154	`stalkTensorDescU` through the germ, by the colimit universal property. -/
155	@[reassoc]
156	lemma germ_stalkTensorDesc (U : Opens X) (hx : x ∈ U) :
157	    TopCat.Presheaf.germ (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf U x hx ≫ stalkTensorDesc A B x
158	      = stalkTensorDescU A B x U hx :=
159	  colimit.ι_desc _ (op (⟨U, hx⟩ : OpenNhds x))
160	
161	/-- **Germ characterisation of the d.2 comparison map** on a simple tensor: the germ of
162	`a ⊗ b` over `U` maps to `germ a ⊗ germ b`. -/
163	@[simp]
164	lemma stalkTensorDesc_germ_tmul (U : Opens X) (hx : x ∈ U)
165	    (a : ↑(A.obj (op U))) (b : ↑(B.obj (op U))) :
166	    (stalkTensorDesc A B x)
167	        ((ConcreteCategory.hom (TopCat.Presheaf.germ (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf U x hx))
168	          (a ⊗ₜ[R.obj (op U)] b))
169	      = (ConcreteCategory.hom (TopCat.Presheaf.germ A.presheaf U x hx)) a ⊗ₜ[↑(R.stalk x)]
170	        (ConcreteCategory.hom (TopCat.Presheaf.germ B.presheaf U x hx)) b := by
171	  rw [← stalkTensorDescU_tmul, ← CategoryTheory.comp_apply, germ_stalkTensorDesc]
172	
173	/-- **Element form of `germ_stalkTensorDesc`.** The comparison map sends the germ of a
174	section `w` over `U` to its per-neighbourhood descent `stalkTensorDescU`. -/
175	lemma stalkTensorDesc_germ (U : Opens X) (hx : x ∈ U)
176	    (w : ↑((Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf.obj (op U))) :
177	    (stalkTensorDesc A B x)
178	        ((ConcreteCategory.hom (TopCat.Presheaf.germ (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf U x hx)) w)
179	      = (stalkTensorDescU A B x U hx) w := by
180	  rw [← CategoryTheory.comp_apply, germ_stalkTensorDesc]
181	
182	/-- **The `R.stalk x`-linear d.2 comparison map** (stage (iii)). The additive comparison
183	map `stalkTensorDesc` is `R.stalk x`-linear for the stalk module structures of
184	`Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk`: linearity is checked on a germ `germ r` of a
185	scalar `r : R(U)` via the germ--scalar compatibility `germ_smul` and the section-level
186	scalar compatibility `stalkTensorDescU_smul`, after passing to a common neighbourhood.
187	This is the linear packaging the d.2 stalk--tensor isomorphism `stalkTensorIso` builds on. -/
188	noncomputable def stalkTensorLinearMap :
189	    (↑(TopCat.Presheaf.stalk (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf x) : Type u)
190	      →ₗ[↑(R.stalk x)] Tgt where
191	  toFun := ConcreteCategory.hom (stalkTensorDesc A B x)
192	  map_add' a b := map_add _ a b
193	  map_smul' r ξ := by
194	    dsimp only [RingHom.id_apply]
195	    obtain ⟨U, hxU, r₀, rfl⟩ := TopCat.Presheaf.germ_exist R x r
196	    obtain ⟨V, hxV, z, rfl⟩ :=
197	      TopCat.Presheaf.germ_exist (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf x ξ
198	    set W : Opens X := U ⊓ V with hW
199	    have hxW : x ∈ W := ⟨hxU, hxV⟩
200	    set iWU : W ⟶ U := homOfLE inf_le_left
201	    set iWV : W ⟶ V := homOfLE inf_le_right
202	    have hr : (ConcreteCategory.hom (R.germ U x hxU)) r₀
203	        = (ConcreteCategory.hom (R.germ W x hxW)) ((ConcreteCategory.hom (R.map iWU.op)) r₀) :=
204	      (TopCat.Presheaf.germ_res_apply R iWU x hxW r₀).symm
205	    have hz : (ConcreteCategory.hom
206	          (TopCat.Presheaf.germ (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf V x hxV)) z
207	        = (ConcreteCategory.hom
208	            (TopCat.Presheaf.germ (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf W x hxW))
209	            ((ConcreteCategory.hom ((Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf.map iWV.op)) z) :=
210	      (TopCat.Presheaf.germ_res_apply (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf iWV x hxW z).symm
211	    rw [hr, hz, ← PresheafOfModules.germ_smul (Monoidal.tensorObj A B) x W hxW,
212	      stalkTensorDesc_germ, stalkTensorDescU_smul, ← stalkTensorDesc_germ]
213	
214	/-- **Germ characterisation of the linear comparison map** on a simple tensor: the germ of
215	`a ⊗ b` over `U` maps to `germ a ⊗ germ b`. The `R.stalk x`-linear repackaging
216	`stalkTensorLinearMap` agrees with `stalkTensorDesc` on germs. -/
217	@[simp]
218	lemma stalkTensorLinearMap_germ_tmul (U : Opens X) (hx : x ∈ U)
219	    (a : ↑(A.obj (op U))) (b : ↑(B.obj (op U))) :
220	    stalkTensorLinearMap A B x
221	        ((ConcreteCategory.hom (TopCat.Presheaf.germ (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf U x hx))
222	          (a ⊗ₜ[R.obj (op U)] b))
223	      = (ConcreteCategory.hom (TopCat.Presheaf.germ A.presheaf U x hx)) a ⊗ₜ[↑(R.stalk x)]
224	        (ConcreteCategory.hom (TopCat.Presheaf.germ B.presheaf U x hx)) b :=
225	  stalkTensorDesc_germ_tmul A B x U hx a b
226	
227	/-! ### Stage (iv): the reverse map -/
228	
229	/-- **Inner cocone leg for the reverse map** (fixed `A`-section `a` over `U`). For a
230	`B`-neighbourhood `V ∋ x`, the additive map `B(V) → (A ⊗ᵖ B).stalk x` sending `b` to the
231	germ of `(a|_{U⊓V}) ⊗ (b|_{U⊓V})`. Project-local building block of the d.2 reverse map. -/
232	private noncomputable def revInnerLeg (U : Opens X) (hxU : x ∈ U) (a : ↑(A.obj (op U)))
233	    (V : Opens X) (hxV : x ∈ V) :
234	    B.presheaf.obj (op V) ⟶ TopCat.Presheaf.stalk (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf x :=
235	  AddCommGrpCat.ofHom <|
236	    (ConcreteCategory.hom
237	        (TopCat.Presheaf.germ (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf (U ⊓ V) x ⟨hxU, hxV⟩)).comp
238	      (((TensorProduct.mk ↑(R.obj (op (U ⊓ V))) ↑(A.obj (op (U ⊓ V))) ↑(B.obj (op (U ⊓ V)))
239	          ((ConcreteCategory.hom (A.presheaf.map (homOfLE inf_le_left : U ⊓ V ⟶ U).op)) a :
240	            ↑(A.obj (op (U ⊓ V))))).toAddMonoidHom).comp
241	        (ConcreteCategory.hom (B.presheaf.map (homOfLE inf_le_right : U ⊓ V ⟶ V).op)))
242	
243	/-- Evaluation of `revInnerLeg`: it sends `b` to the germ of `(a|_{U⊓V}) ⊗ (b|_{U⊓V})`. -/
244	private lemma revInnerLeg_apply (U : Opens X) (hxU : x ∈ U) (a : ↑(A.obj (op U)))
245	    (V : Opens X) (hxV : x ∈ V) (b : ↑(B.presheaf.obj (op V))) :
246	    (ConcreteCategory.hom (revInnerLeg A B x U hxU a V hxV)) b
247	      = (ConcreteCategory.hom
248	          (TopCat.Presheaf.germ (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf (U ⊓ V) x ⟨hxU, hxV⟩))
249	          (((ConcreteCategory.hom (A.presheaf.map (homOfLE inf_le_left : U ⊓ V ⟶ U).op)) a :
250	              ↑(A.obj (op (U ⊓ V)))) ⊗ₜ[R.obj (op (U ⊓ V))]
251	            ((ConcreteCategory.hom (B.presheaf.map (homOfLE inf_le_right : U ⊓ V ⟶ V).op)) b :
252	              ↑(B.obj (op (U ⊓ V))))) :=
253	  rfl
254	
255	/-- **Inner descent of the reverse map** (fixed `A`-section `a` over `U`): the additive
256	map `B.stalk x → (A ⊗ᵖ B).stalk x` descending `revInnerLeg` through the `B`-stalk colimit. -/
257	private noncomputable def revInner (U : Opens X) (hxU : x ∈ U) (a : ↑(A.obj (op U))) :
258	    TopCat.Presheaf.stalk B.presheaf x ⟶
259	      TopCat.Presheaf.stalk (Monoidal.tensorObj A B).presheaf x :=

6	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
7	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
8	
9	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
10	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
11	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
12	\[
13	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
14	  \qquad
15	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
16	\]
17	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
18	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
19	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
20	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
21	
22	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
23	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
24	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
25	% groups)
26	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
27	% SOURCE QUOTE:
28	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
29	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
30	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
31	% abelian groups defined by the formula
32	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
33	% \end{dfn}
34	%  [\dots]
35	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
36	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
37	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
38	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
39	% topologies  by
40	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
41	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
42	%  \end{dfn}"
43	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
44	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
45	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
46	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
47	homomorphisms.}
48	
49	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
50	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
51	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
52	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
53	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
54	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
55	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
56	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
57	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
58	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
59	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
60	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
61	structure. This is the content of
62	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
63	
64	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
65	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
66	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
67	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
68	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
69	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
70	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
71	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
72	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
73	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
74	\begin{enumerate}
75	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
76	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
77	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
78	    isomorphism classes;
79	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
80	    for \(\otimes\);
81	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
82	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
83	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
84	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
85	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
86	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
87	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
88	\end{enumerate}
89	At Mathlib's pinned commit one might be tempted to obtain (3) by instantiating a
90	\emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) and
91	reading the three isomorphisms off it. Two Mathlib facts make such a route
92	conceivable. First, the category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves
93	of modules over a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) is already a (symmetric)
94	monoidal category in Mathlib (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\)),
95	with the sectionwise relative tensor \((M \otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \otimes_{R(U)}
96	N(U)\); this applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
97	\(R = \mathcal{O}_X\). Second, Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
98	(\(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)) takes a
99	monoidal category \(C\), a localization functor \(L : C \to D\) at a morphism
100	property \(W\), and an instance \(W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and produces a
101	\emph{full} monoidal-category structure on the localization \(D\); sheafification
102	of presheaves of modules is the localization \(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the
103	locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\), so a full monoidal structure on
104	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) would follow once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) were
105	supplied.
106	
107	This full-monoidal-category route is, however, \emph{not} the goal, and is not
108	pursued. The goal is the commutative \emph{group} on locally-trivial iso-classes
109	(à la \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)), and that group law consumes only propositions, so
110	it never needs a coherent monoidal category --- neither the
111	pentagon/triangle/hexagon identities nor the \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance
112	that the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) API requires. What it needs is exactly the
113	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms of (3), and these are produced
114	directly: the associator by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
115	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
116	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), the unitors by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\)
117	pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), and the braiding likewise
118	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). With those three existence facts the group law
119	assembles \emph{by hand} the commutative group of \(\otimes\)-invertible
120	\(\otimes\)-iso-classes --- the Picard group
121	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) --- each group-axiom field fed a single
122	existence-of-isomorphism. This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
123	fixed-ring \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\),
124	whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent
125	monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) that
126	Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring; the present construction cannot take that
127	\(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route, since the coherent monoidal category on
128	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is exactly what it
129	avoids. The
130	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
131	Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom) carries each inverse existentially.
132	The \emph{full} \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) / \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) coherent
133	monoidal category is therefore not built for the group law and is off the critical
134	path; what the group law does still need from this circle of ideas is the
135	\emph{existence} of the associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), realized as a
136	sheafification transport whose single open left-whisker obligation is closed by the
137	stalk--tensor commutation (d.2). The stalk apparatus (d.1/d.2) is accordingly
138	\emph{on} the critical path, while the coherent monoidal category itself is not.
139	
140	\section{Mathlib API survey}
141	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
142	
143	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
144	(\texttt{b80f227}) were originally assembled into a single \emph{realization route}
145	(route~(e)): obtaining a full monoidal structure on the substrate by instantiating
146	Mathlib's abstract localization-of-monoidal-categories API rather than
147	hand-assembling the associator. That route is \emph{superseded}. The group law on
148	iso-classes never consumes a coherent monoidal category, and the associator is
149	obtained by direct gluing through \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, so the
150	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance on which route~(e) hinges is never required.
151	The survey below is retained as a Mathlib-API reference and to record why the route
152	is vestigial; the four Mathlib facts are all verified against the on-disk Mathlib
153	source.

299	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
300	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
301	
302	\begin{definition}
303	
304	
305	\leanok
306	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
307	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
308	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
309	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
310	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
311	  \[
312	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
313	  \]
314	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
315	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
316	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
317	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
318	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
319	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
320	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
321	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
322	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
323	  canonical compatibility
324	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
325	     \;\cong\;
326	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
327	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
328	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
329	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
330	
331	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
332	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
333	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
334	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
335	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
336	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
337	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
338	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
339	\end{definition}
340	
341	\begin{lemma}
342	
343	
344	\leanok
345	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
346	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
347	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
348	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
349	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
350	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
351	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
352	  \[
353	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
354	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
355	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
356	  \]
357	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
358	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
359	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
360	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
361	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
362	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
363	  \[
364	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
365	    \qquad
366	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
367	  \]
368	  the associator
369	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
370	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
371	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
372	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
373	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
374	\end{lemma}
375	
376	\begin{proof}
377	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
378	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
379	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
380	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
381	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
382	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
383	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
384	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
385	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
386	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
387	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
388	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
389	\end{proof}
390	
391	\begin{remark}
392	[The full monoidal category on all of $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$ is off-path; the group law consumes only propositions]
393	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
394	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
395	        lem:whisker_of_W}
396	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
397	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
398	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
399	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
400	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
401	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This is explicitly \emph{off-path}
402	  and \emph{not pursued}. One could in principle obtain it as the output of
403	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
404	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
405	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), supplying the instance
406	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}); but the group law never
407	  consumes a coherent monoidal category, so this construction is superseded and not
408	  carried out. The substrate provides only the \emph{existence} of the three
409	  coherence isomorphisms that the group law actually consumes.
410	
411	  Were that superseded route pursued, the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\)
412	  would need \emph{no} closed structure: its
413	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
414	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
415	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
416	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
417	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\)), the same technique Mathlib uses for
418	  fixed-base presheaves in
419	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\). It would use neither
420	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
421	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
422	  false over a non-affine open and is likewise off the critical path). The \emph{full}
423	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance and the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal
424	  category are not on the critical path: the group law is realized without them. The
425	  single left-whisker existence lemma and its underlying d.1/d.2 stalk apparatus, by
426	  contrast, \emph{are} on the critical path, since the associator's sheafification
427	  transport consumes exactly that one whisker obligation
428	  (\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, closed via d.2).
429	
430	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
431	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
432	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
433	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
434	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
435	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
436	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
437	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
438	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
439	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, supplied directly: the associator by gluing canonical
440	  local isomorphisms through the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
441	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), and the
442	  unitors and braiding by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern
443	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). The carrier --- iso-classes
444	  of the substrate tensor \(\otimes_X\) --- is the scheme-theoretic analogue of
445	  Mathlib's Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
446	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}), but it
447	  is built \emph{differently}: Mathlib reads its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) off the
448	  \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent monoidal category
449	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) over a fixed ring,
450	  whereas here --- with no coherent monoidal category on
451	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) available for the varying structure sheaf --- the
452	  group is assembled by hand from the existence-of-isomorphism facts.
453	\end{remark}

455	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
456	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
457	
458	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
459	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
460	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
461	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
462	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
463	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
464	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes. These facts
465	are supplied \emph{directly}: associativity by the direct-gluing associator
466	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (through the linchpin
467	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), unit and commutativity by the cheap
468	\(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}),
469	and the inverse existentially from \(\otimes\)-invertibility. They are \emph{not}
470	read off any \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure: the route that would derive them from
471	\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} (route~(e)) is superseded and off-path. The section also
472	records the closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial
473	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and
474	the interaction with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion
475	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is on the
476	critical path precisely because it is consumed by the direct-gluing associator
477	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}; it is \emph{not} tied to the superseded
478	whiskering-stability lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, which belongs
479	to the off-path route~(e).
480	
481	\begin{lemma}
482	
483	\leanok
484	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
485	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
486	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
487	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
488	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
489	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
490	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
491	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
492	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
493	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
494	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
495	  % critical path for the tensor group law.
496	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
497	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
498	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
499	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
500	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
501	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
502	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
503	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
504	  \[
505	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
506	      \;\;:=\;\;
507	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
508	  \]
509	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
510	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
511	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
512	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
513	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
514	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
515	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.
516	\end{lemma}
517	
518	\begin{proof}
519	
520	
521	\leanok
522	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
523	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
524	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
525	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
526	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
527	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
528	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
529	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
530	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
531	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
532	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
533	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
534	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
535	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
536	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
537	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
538	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
539	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
540	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
541	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq
542	  transport across the definitional unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
543	
544	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
545	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
546	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
547	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
548	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
549	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
550	\end{proof}
551	
552	\begin{lemma}
553	
554	
555	\leanok
556	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
557	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
558	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
559	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
560	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
561	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
562	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
563	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
564	  morphism
565	  \[
566	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
567	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
568	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
569	  \]
570	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
571	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
572	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
573	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
574	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
575	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.
576	\end{lemma}
577	
578	\begin{proof}
579	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
580	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
581	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
582	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
583	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
584	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
585	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
586	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
587	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
588	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
589	  isomorphism}
590	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
591	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
592	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
593	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
594	  therefore extension of scalars along
595	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
596	
597	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
598	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
599	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
600	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
601	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
602	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
603	  the abstract pullback functor.
604	
605	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
606	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
607	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
608	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
609	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
610	   \;\cong\;
611	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
612	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
613	  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
614	  comparison
615	  \[
616	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
617	      \;\longrightarrow\;
618	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
619	      \;\otimes\;
620	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
621	  \]
622	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).
623	
624	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
625	  % \ref{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback} (the pair $(f^*, f_*)$ of
626	  % pullback/pushforward of $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a morphism of ringed
627	  % spaces is an adjoint pair)
628	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L252-273).
629	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
630	  % "\begin{lemma}
631	  % \label{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}
632	  % Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$
633	  % be a morphism of ringed spaces.
634	  % \begin{enumerate}
635	  % \item The functor
636	  % $f_* : \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \to \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_Y)$
637	  % is left exact. In fact it commutes with all limits.
638	  % \item The functor
639	  % $f^* : \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_Y) \to \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$
640	  % is right exact. In fact it commutes with all colimits.
641	  %  [\dots]
642	  % \end{enumerate}
643	  % \end{lemma}
644	  %  [\dots]
645	  % Parts (1) and (2) hold because $(f^*, f_*)$ is an adjoint pair
646	  % of functors, see
647	  % Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-adjoint-pullback-pushforward-modules}"
648	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
649	  \texttt{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}: pullback and pushforward of
650	  $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a morphism form an adjoint pair $(f^*, f_*)$, the
651	  abstract universal property on which the comparison below rests.}
652	
653	  \emph{Step 3 (the genuine residual: H1 alone).} Completing this comparison at the
654	  presheaf level requires a sectionwise handle on the abstract functor
655	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), which is defined as the left
656	  adjoint of pushforward (the presheaf-of-modules avatar of the
657	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) adjunction of the cited Stacks lemma) and admits no
658	  sectionwise formula in Mathlib at the pinned commit. The closure passes through
659	  two presheaf-level ingredients, of which exactly one now remains open.
660	
661	  The first ingredient --- a strong-monoidal upgrade of
662	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along the structure-sheaf ring \emph{isomorphism}
663	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\), so that base change along the isomorphism commutes with
664	  \(\otimes\) --- is \emph{closed in this chapter} as
665	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
666	  (\(\mathtt{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}\)): for a ring isomorphism
667	  \(e : R \simeq S\) the canonical map \(a \otimes_R b \mapsto a \otimes_S b\) is an
668	  \(R\)-linear equivalence \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R
669	  \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M
670	  \otimes_S N)\).
671	
672	  The \emph{sole remaining residual} is therefore the presheaf-level identification
673	  \[
674	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \;\cong\; \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
675	  \]
676	  via \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\). One exhibits a
677	  presheaf-level adjunction
678	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \dashv \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) and concludes
679	  that \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\), being a left adjoint of
680	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\), is canonically isomorphic to the abstract left
681	  adjoint \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\). Here \(\beta\) is the structure map of the
682	  restriction functor, so that \((M|_f).\mathrm{val} =
683	  (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathrm{obj}\,M.\mathrm{val}\) definitionally;
684	  composing the resulting comparison with
685	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv} yields the asserted isomorphism.
686	
687	  This presheaf-level adjunction is the presheaf analogue of the sheaf-level
688	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\), and must be built from
689	  presheaf-level \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatTrans}\) and
690	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardCongr}\). These two building blocks are \emph{Mathlib-absent}
691	  at the pinned commit: only the \emph{sheaf}-level versions exist
692	  (\texttt{Mathlib/.../SheafOfModules/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean}), whereas
693	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardId}\) and \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\) do already exist at the
694	  presheaf level. This residual is being constructed \emph{directly}, mirroring the
695	  existing sheaf-level template app-for-app at the presheaf level (a Mathlib-gradient
696	  build, not a deferral to an upstream PR).
697	
698	  This lemma is the single substrate linchpin of the relative Picard group law for
699	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(M, N\): the associator
700	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} consumes the comparison
701	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_f \cong M|_f \otimes_U N|_f\) on its sole consumer
702	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}, where the restriction is commuted
703	  past the \emph{sheafified} tensor \(M \otimes_X N\) \emph{before} any local
704	  trivialisation of \(M, N\) is introduced --- so the comparison is required for the
705	  global, untrivialised modules, and no free-cover specialisation can stand in for it.
706	  Consequently the H1 residual \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong
707	  \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) is genuinely on the critical path, and closing it is
708	  the \emph{single open substrate obligation} of this chapter. The superseded
709	  route~(e) \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal} \to \mathtt{Sheaf.monoidalCategory}\)
710	  (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}) remains named only as a last-resort fallback should the
711	  presheaf-level adjunction itself bottom out; it is not the plan.
712	\end{proof}

717	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.pushforwardNatTrans,
718	        PresheafOfModules.pushforwardCongr,
719	        PresheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj,

725	  the pinned commit and are supplied project-side.

728	  rings, \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatTrans}\) and \(\mathtt{pushforwardCongr}\) are the
729	  presheaf-level de-sheafifications of Mathlib's sheaf-level
730	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardNatTrans}\) and
731	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardCongr}\)
732	  (\texttt{Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean}),

737	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\). Pairing this against the
738	  existing \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction}\) through
739	  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\) yields the canonical isomorphism
740	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) of two left
741	  adjoints of the same pushforward --- exactly the residual identified in Step~3 of
742	  the proof of \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.

744	  \emph{H2 (strong-monoidal restriction).} \(\mathtt{isIso\_of\_isIso\_app}\) is the
745	  sectionwise isomorphism criterion: a morphism of presheaves of modules is an
746	  isomorphism once each of its section maps is. Built on it,

748	  restriction-of-scalars functor
749	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\alpha\) with a \emph{strong}-monoidal

751	  bijective} --- the presheaf-level lift of the ModuleCat-level H2 core
752	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}, assembled section-by-section via

754	  module-level, ring-equivalence form
755	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalarsMonoidalOfRingEquiv}\) already pinned in

759	  \((\alpha.\mathtt{app}\,X).\mathtt{hom}\) is a bijective \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)

762	  Together H1 and H2 are the two Mathlib-absent ingredients the linchpin needed; they

769	[Base change along a ring isomorphism commutes with $\otimes$]
770	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
771	  \lean{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}
772	  Let \(e : R \simeq S\) be an isomorphism of commutative rings, and let \(M, N\) be
773	  \(S\)-modules. Restriction of scalars along \(e\) commutes with the tensor
774	  product: the canonical map
775	  \[
776	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N)
777	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
778	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\bigl(M \otimes_S N\bigr),
779	    \qquad a \otimes_R b \;\longmapsto\; a \otimes_S b,
780	  \]
781	  is an \(R\)-linear equivalence. Equivalently, \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\) is
782	  \emph{strong} monoidal, not merely lax, when \(e\) is a ring \emph{isomorphism}:
783	  base change along an isomorphism \(R \simeq S\) identifies the two ground rings, so
784	  the lax structure map of \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\) is invertible. The
785	  forward map is \(R\)-linear, but its inverse \(a \otimes_S b \mapsto a \otimes_R b\)
786	  is only \emph{additive} (it is not \(S\)-linear), so the inverse is constructed as
787	  a homomorphism of additive groups and then verified to be the two-sided inverse of
788	  the forward \(R\)-linear map. This closes the strong-monoidal ingredient of
789	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, leaving the presheaf-level pushforward
790	  adjunction as the sole open residual there.
791	\end{lemma}

871	[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
872	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
873	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial}
874	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
875	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
876	  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
877	  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
878	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
879	  one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) restricts to a bifunctor
880	  on the full subcategory \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X \subset \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
881	  of locally-trivial rank-one modules.
882	\end{lemma}
883	
884	\begin{proof}
885	
886	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
887	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
888	  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
889	  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
890	  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
891	  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
892	  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of
893	  \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
894	\end{proof}

914	[Flat whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
915	  \label{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
916	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
917	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
918	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the class of morphisms of presheaves of
919	  \(R\)-modules that are inverted by sheafification --- equivalently, the
920	  \emph{locally bijective} morphisms, i.e.\ those that are both locally injective
921	  and locally surjective for \(J\) (the characterisation
922	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). Let \(F\) be a presheaf of \(R\)-modules
923	  that is \emph{flat} (sectionwise: \(F(U)\) is a flat \(R(U)\)-module for every
924	  object \(U\) of the site). Then for every morphism \(g\) in \(J.W\) the
925	  left-whiskered morphism
926	  \[
927	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
928	  \]
929	  (the action of \(F \otimes^{p} -\) on \(g\), with \(\otimes^{p}\) the
930	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product) again lies in \(J.W\). By the symmetry of
931	  the presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, the right-whiskered morphism
932	  \(g \triangleright F\) likewise lies in \(J.W\) when \(F\) is flat.
933	
934	  Both halves --- the left-whiskering statement and its braiding-conjugate
935	  right-whiskering statement --- are established. They are valid standalone
936	  results about flat whiskering of sheafification-localizer morphisms. They are,
937	  however, \emph{off the critical path}: the live whiskering-stability obligation
938	  for the unconditional associator is closed for an \emph{arbitrary} whiskering
939	  object \(F\) by the stalk--tensor commutation
940	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}
941	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}), which never invokes the
942	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of the present lemma. The sectionwise flatness
943	  condition --- \(F(U)\) flat over \(R(U)\) for \emph{every} object \(U\) of the
944	  site, including non-affine opens --- is not supplied by \(\otimes\)-invertibility
945	  or local triviality, which are affine-local properties; the present (flat) lemma
946	  is therefore retained as an independent, closed result, superseded on the critical
947	  path by the d.2 route of \cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}.

950	\begin{proof}
951	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
952	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of the sheafification localizer
953	  (\(J.W\) is exactly the class of morphisms that are \(J\)-locally injective and
954	  \(J\)-locally surjective), it suffices to show that \(F \triangleleft g\) is
955	  both locally injective and locally surjective whenever \(g\) is.
956	
957	  The presheaf-of-modules tensor product is computed sectionwise,
958	  \((F \otimes^{p} G)(U) = F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} G(U)\), so for
959	  \(g : A \to B\) the whiskered morphism acts on sections over \(U\) as the
960	  \(R(U)\)-linear map
961	  \[
962	    \mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U) \;\colon\;
963	      F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} A(U) \;\longrightarrow\; F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U).
964	  \]
965	
966	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is automatic and needs no hypothesis on \(F\):
967	  tensoring is right exact, so applying \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the cokernel
968	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\); since \(g\) is locally
969	  surjective its cokernel is locally zero, hence so is the cokernel of
970	  \(F \triangleleft g\), and \(F \triangleleft g\) is locally surjective.
971	
972	  \emph{Local injectivity} is where flatness enters. Because each \(F(U)\) is a
973	  flat \(R(U)\)-module, the functor \(F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} -\) preserves injective
974	  linear maps (Mathlib
975	  \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)). Thus
976	  whenever \(g(U)\) is injective on a covering sieve, so is
977	  \((\mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U))\); the local injectivity of \(g\) is
978	  therefore transported sectionwise to \(F \triangleleft g\).
979	
980	  Locally injective and locally surjective together give
981	  \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
982	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
983	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, which is an
984	  isomorphism and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\); the flatness of \(F\) is
985	  the only input, and it is symmetric in the two tensor factors.
986	\end{proof}

1901	\begin{proof}
1902	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map}
1903	  The stalk at \(x\) is the filtered colimit over the neighbourhoods \(U \ni x\) of
1904	  the sections functor, and sectionwise the tensor product is computed pointwise:
1905	  \((A \otimes^{p} B)(U) = A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U)\)
1906	  (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj\_obj}\)). The neighbourhood system
1907	  \(\{U \ni x\}\) is cofiltered under inclusion, so the colimit is filtered. The
1908	  isomorphism is assembled in five named stages. Stages (i)--(ii) construct the
1909	  forward additive comparison map and its germ characterisation; stages (iii)--(v)
1910	  upgrade it to an \(R_x\)-linear map, construct the reverse map, and check mutual
1911	  inversion.
1912	
1913	  \emph{(i) Per-neighbourhood balanced bilinear map and its section-level descent}
1914	  (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorBilin}\), \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDescU}\)). For a fixed
1915	  neighbourhood \(U \ni x\) the assignment
1916	  \[
1917	    (a, b) \;\longmapsto\; \mathrm{germ}_x\,a \,\otimes\, \mathrm{germ}_x\,b,
1918	    \qquad a \in A(U),\ b \in B(U),
1919	  \]
1920	  is \(R(U)\)-balanced bilinear into \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x\): it is additive in each
1921	  variable, and for \(r \in R(U)\) the two ways of moving \(r\) across the tensor agree
1922	  after passing to germs, since \(\mathrm{germ}_x(r \cdot a) = (\mathrm{germ}_x r)
1923	  \cdot \mathrm{germ}_x a\) and the stalk tensor is balanced over
1924	  \(R_x = R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\) (the germ--scalar compatibility of
1925	  \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}). By the universal property of the relative tensor
1926	  product it descends to an additive map on the section module
1927	  \[
1928	    \mathtt{stalkTensorDescU}_U \;\colon\;
1929	      (A \otimes^{p} B)(U) = A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U)
1930	        \;\longrightarrow\; A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x,
1931	    \qquad a \otimes b \longmapsto \mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes \mathrm{germ}_x b.
1932	  \]
1933	
1934	  \emph{(ii) Colimit descent to the forward comparison map}
1935	  (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\), \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc\_germ\_tmul}\)). The
1936	  maps \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDescU}_U\) are compatible with the restriction maps of the
1937	  presheaf \(A \otimes^{p} B\) as \(U\) shrinks toward \(x\) (a smaller neighbourhood
1938	  computes the same germs), so they form a cocone over the filtered diagram
1939	  \(\{(A \otimes^{p} B)(U)\}_{U \ni x}\). The induced map out of the colimit is the
1940	  forward additive comparison map
1941	  \[
1942	    \mathtt{stalkTensorDesc} \;\colon\;
1943	      (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x \;\longrightarrow\; A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x,
1944	  \]
1945	  characterised on germs of simple tensors by
1946	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\bigl(\mathrm{germ}_x(a \otimes b)\bigr) =
1947	  \mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes \mathrm{germ}_x b\)
1948	  (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc\_germ\_tmul}\)). This is the forward half of the asserted
1949	  isomorphism (\cref{lem:stalk_tensor_desc_forward}).
1950	
1951	  \emph{(iii) \(R_x\)-linearity packaging} (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorLinearMap}\)). The
1952	  additive map of (ii) is upgraded to an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear map: on a germ
1953	  \(\mathrm{germ}_x r\) of a section \(r \in R(U)\) one checks
1954	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\bigl((\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \xi\bigr) =
1955	  (\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}(\xi)\) on germs of simple tensors
1956	  and extends by additivity. The one subtlety is a \emph{carrier-duality obstacle}:
1957	  the section-level tensor \(A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U)\) is a module over the
1958	  \(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carrier \((R \circ \mathtt{forget_2})(U)\), whereas the natural
1959	  scalar \(r : R(U)\) lives in the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) carrier \(R(U)\); the two
1960	  carriers are canonically identified but not definitionally equal, so the
1961	  scalar-pulling lemma \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.smul\_tmul'}\) applies only after
1962	  inserting a canonical \(\mathtt{RingEquiv}\) bridge between the
1963	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and \(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carriers. Once that bridge is
1964	  in place the linearity computation mirrors the germ-representative pattern used
1965	  for the d.1 stalk map \(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\) (\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}).
1966	
1967	  \emph{(iv) The reverse map.} The map
1968	  \[
1969	    A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x \;\longrightarrow\; (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x
1970	  \]
1971	  is built by the universal property of the tensor product over \(R_x\) from the
1972	  \(R_x\)-bilinear map of the two stalks
1973	  \[
1974	    (\mathrm{germ}_x a,\ \mathrm{germ}_x b) \;\longmapsto\;
1975	      \mathrm{germ}_x\bigl(a|_{U \cap V} \otimes b|_{U \cap V}\bigr),
1976	  \]
1977	  where \(a \in A(U)\) and \(b \in B(V)\) are chosen representatives; this is a
1978	  \emph{nested} colimit descent, descending first the \(A\)-variable out of the
1979	  filtered colimit \(\{A(U)\}_{U \ni x}\) and then the \(B\)-variable out of
1980	  \(\{B(V)\}_{V \ni x}\), passing to the common refinement \(U \cap V\) to form the
1981	  simple tensor and its germ. Well-definedness on germs (independence of the chosen
1982	  representatives and of the neighbourhoods \(U, V\)) is exactly filteredness of the
1983	  neighbourhood system, and \(R_x\)-bilinearity reduces to the germ--scalar
1984	  compatibility of (i)/(iii).
1985	
1986	  \emph{(v) Mutual inversion on germ generators} (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorIso}\)). The two
1987	  composites are checked to be identities on generators. The composite
1988	  \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x \to (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x \to A_x
1989	  \otimes_{R_x} B_x\) is the identity on a simple tensor \(\mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes
1990	  \mathrm{germ}_x b\) by the germ characterisation \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc\_germ\_tmul}\)
1991	  of (ii), and extends to all of \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x\) by
1992	  \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.induction\_on}\). The opposite composite
1993	  \((A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x \to A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x \to (A \otimes^{p}
1994	  B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x\) is the identity on a germ \(\mathrm{germ}_x(a \otimes b)\) of
1995	  a simple tensor by the same characterisation; since germs generate the stalk and the
1996	  germ maps are jointly epimorphic (\(\mathtt{stalk\_hom\_ext}\)), together with
1997	  \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.induction\_on}\) applied to each section, the composite is the
1998	  identity. Bundling the \(R_x\)-linear forward map of (iii) with the reverse map of
1999	  (iv) and these two inversion identities yields the asserted isomorphism
2000	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorIso}\).
2001	
2002	  Naturality in \(A, B\) is inherited from naturality of the sectionwise tensor and of
2003	  the germ maps. This is the presheaf-of-modules, varying-ring analogue of the Stacks
2004	  lemma above; the only subtlety beyond the ringed-space statement is that the ground
2005	  ring varies with \(U\), handled by the ``tensor of filtered colimits over the colimit

2007	  the tensor product of modules commutes with filtered colimits in each variable and
2008	  the ground ring \(R(U)\) itself colimits to the stalk ring \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\),
2009	  which is the reason the forward map (ii) and the reverse map (iv) are mutually
2010	  inverse.
2011	\end{proof}
